Home   Sun, Moon & Space   Eclipses   August 12, 2026 Total Solar Eclipse

August 12, 2026 Total Solar Eclipse

Eclipse InformationPath Map3D Path GlobeLive Stream

The first total solar eclipse for mainland Europe since 1999 is visible from parts of Russia, Greenland, Iceland, and Spain.

Watch the eclipse LIVE!

This eclipse is visible in Wheaton - go to local timings and animation

What the Eclipse Will Look Like near the Maximum Point

The animation shows what the eclipse approximately looks like near the maximum point. The curvature of the Moon's path is due to the Earth's rotation.

Where to See the Eclipse

Try our new interactive eclipse maps. Zoom in and search for accurate eclipse times and visualizations for any location.

Path of the Eclipse Shadow

Regions seeing, at least, a partial eclipse: Europe, North in Asia, North/West Africa, Much of North America, Pacific, Atlantic, Arctic.

Expand for a list of selected cities where at least part of the total eclipse is visible
  • Station Nord, Greenland
  • Ísafjörður, Iceland
  • Borgarnes, Iceland
  • Reykjavik, Iceland
  • Keflavík, Iceland
  • Santander, Cantabria, Spain
  • Bilbao, Vizcaya, Spain
  • Gijón, Asturias, Spain
  • Oviedo, Asturias, Spain
  • Vitoria-Gasteiz, Álava, Spain
  • A Coruña, A Coruña, Spain
  • Lugo, Lugo, Spain
  • Logroño, La Rioja, Spain
  • Burgos, Burgos, Spain
  • Ponferrada, León, Spain
  • Zaragoza, Zaragoza, Spain
  • Palencia, Palencia, Spain
  • Salou, Tarragona, Spain
  • Valladolid, Valladolid, Spain
  • Mahón, Menorca, Spain
  • Port de Pollença, Balearic Islands, Spain
  • Alcúdia, Baleares, Spain
  • Segovia, Segovia, Spain
  • Teruel, Teruel, Spain
  • Palma, Majorca, Spain
  • Guadalajara, Guadalajara, Spain
  • Cuenca, Cuenca, Spain
  • Valencia, Valencia, Spain
  • Sant Antoni de Portmany, Ibiza, Spain
  • Ibiza, Ibiza, Spain
Expand for a list of selected cities where the partial eclipse is visible

  • Longyearbyen, Svalbard, Norway
  • Danmarkshavn, Greenland

  • Vestmannaeyjar, Iceland

  • Brussels, Brussels, Belgium
  • Luxembourg, Luxembourg
  • Paris, Paris, France
  • Zürich, Zurich, Switzerland
  • Monaco, Monaco
  • Andorra La Vella, Andorra

  • Lisbon, Lisbon, Portugal
  • Gibraltar, Gibraltar, Gibraltar
  • Casablanca, Morocco

This eclipse is visible in Wheaton - go to local timings and animation

Eclipse Shadow Path

When the Eclipse Happens Worldwide — Timeline

The eclipse starts at one location and ends at another. The times below are actual times (in UTC) when the eclipse occurs. This calculation uses a Delta T value of 69.6 seconds.

Eclipse Stages WorldwideUTC TimeLocal Time in Wheaton*
First location to see the partial eclipse beginAug 12 at 15:34:15Aug 12 at 10:34:15 am
First location to see the full eclipse beginAug 12 at 16:58:09Aug 12 at 11:58:09 am
Maximum EclipseAug 12 at 17:46:06Aug 12 at 12:46:06 pm
Last location to see the full eclipse endAug 12 at 18:34:07Aug 12 at 1:34:07 pm
Last location to see the partial eclipse endAug 12 at 19:57:57Aug 12 at 2:57:57 pm

* These local times do not refer to a specific location but indicate the beginning, peak, and end of the eclipse on a global scale, each line referring to a different location. Please note that the local times for Wheaton are meant as a guideline in case you want to view the eclipse via a live webcam. See the actual times the eclipse is visible in Wheaton.

Upcoming eclipses visible in Wheaton

Next Total Solar Eclipse will be on Aug 2, 2027

Eclipse calculations usually accurate to a few seconds

All times shown in this table are local time. (Note: more than one time zone is listed.)

How Many People Can See This Eclipse?

Number of People Seeing...Number of People*Fraction of World Population
Any part of the eclipse980,000,00012.08%
At least 10% partial779,000,0009.60%
At least 20% partial714,000,0008.80%
At least 30% partial671,000,0008.27%
At least 40% partial627,000,0007.73%
At least 50% partial602,000,0007.42%
At least 60% partial581,000,0007.16%
At least 70% partial562,000,0006.93%
At least 80% partial521,000,0006.42%
At least 90% partial283,000,0003.49%
Totality or annularity15,200,0000.19%

* The number of people refers to the resident population (as a round number) in areas where the eclipse is visible. timeanddate has calculated these numbers using raw population data provided by the Center for International Earth Science Information Network (CIESIN) at Columbia University. The raw data is based on population estimates from the year 2000 to 2020.

An Eclipse Never Comes Alone!

A solar eclipse always occurs about two weeks before or after a lunar eclipse.

Usually, there are two eclipses in a row, but other times, there are three during the same eclipse season.

All eclipses 1900 — 2199

This is the first eclipse this season.

Second eclipse this season: August 28, 2026 — Partial Lunar Eclipse

How accurate is the eclipse calculation

Total Solar Eclipse Countdown

Aug 12, 2026 at 15:34 UTC

Lunar Eclipse Live Show

We are streaming the total lunar eclipse on Mar 2–3 LIVE

Eclipses in 2026

  • Feb 17, 2026 – Annular Solar Eclipse
  • Mar 2–3, 2026 — Total Lunar Eclipse
  • Aug 12, 2026 – Total Solar Eclipse (this page)
  • Aug 27–28, 2026 — Partial Lunar Eclipse

Eclipses in 2027

  • Feb 6, 2027 – Annular Solar Eclipse
  • Feb 20–21, 2027 — Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
  • Jul 18–19, 2027 — Almost Lunar Eclipse
  • Aug 2, 2027 – Total Solar Eclipse
  • Aug 16–17, 2027 — Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
